meta.description: Organizes biomedical figures, analyses, and result blocks into a clear Results section structure with disciplined narrative ordering and evidence-aware presentation.

scientific_integrity.detail: No fabricated figures, results, cohort details, or PMIDs produced. Citation-support annotation provides PubMed search queries, not invented references. Hard rules 1 and 7 are explicit and consistently enforced.

practice_boundaries.detail: No diagnostic or prescriptive clinical conclusions. Skill is limited to structural organization; hard rule 6 prevents Discussion-style interpretation from entering Results.

methodological_ground.detail: Ordering logic correctly prioritizes primary findings over exploratory analyses. Hard rule 4 prevents promotion of exploratory to primary. Section H (Claim Boundary Check) enforces evidence-level constraints.

code_usability.detail: Mode A skill — no code generated.

functional_suitability.note: Covers ten study types including single-cell, multi-omics, and MR/QTL. Nine-step workflow and nine-section output (A–I) are complete and well-matched. Citation-support annotation with opt-out mechanism is unique and well-implemented. Upload recommendation rule addresses the case where structured input is unavailable.

reliability.note: Clarification-first gate + upload recommendation rule provide two independent input-sufficiency checks. Section C explicitly names organizational problems found. Minor deduction: no partial-results pathway when user insists on proceeding with minimal figure inventory.

performance_context.note: Seven compact reference files (5–18 lines each). SKILL.md 275 lines. Minor deduction: Section C (main problems) and Section D (recommended structure) have partial content overlap — both describe what is wrong and what should change.

agent_usability.note: Full marks. Six sample triggers, eight-item core function list, quality standard comparison. Nine fixed A–I section labels ensure consistent structure. Error prevention via clarification-first rule, ten hard rules, 'not for' list, and 'important distinctions' section.

human_usability.note: Six sample triggers and quality standard comparison make entry points very clear. Section I and upload recommendation tell users exactly what to provide next. Minor deduction: no explicit restart path when user provides additional figures after partial structuring begins.

security.note: No credentials, APIs, or code execution. Hard rules 1 and 7 prevent fabricating results, figures, or PMIDs. Citation-support annotation is PubMed query only — no invented references. Hard rule 8 includes explicit opt-out for citation annotation.

maintainability.note: Seven focused reference files; adding a new study type (e.g., spatial transcriptomics) requires only updating results-ordering-rules.md. Clean separation between ordering logic, boundary rules, and citation rules. Minor deduction: no worked example showing how multi-omics layers should be ordered.

agent_specific.note: Trigger precision: six specific triggers plus 'not for' scoping. Progressive disclosure: clarification gate + upload recommendation + Section A + Section I. Idempotency: A–I structure stable across identical inputs. Escape hatches: Section I + upload recommendation + Section H claim boundary check (unique escape hatch that explicitly states what the structure must NOT imply). Deduction: no explicit composability with results-section-writer for downstream prose generation (2/4 composability).

inputs.label: Cohort study with 5 figures in fragmented order, primary result buried in figure 3
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Fragmented order diagnosed. Primary result correctly moved forward. Cohort flow → characteristics → primary → subgroup → validation order recommended.

assertions.text: Output correctly identifies the buried primary result as the key organizational problem
assertions.note: Section C explicitly names 'primary finding buried in Figure 3' as the main structural weakness.

assertions.text: Output recommends opening with cohort characteristics before primary findings
assertions.note: Descriptive setup (cohort flow + characteristics) correctly placed first per results-ordering-rules.md.

assertions.text: Output does not invent additional figures to fill structural gaps
assertions.note: Hard rule 1 holds — structure built from the five provided figures only.

assertions.text: Section H states what the Results structure must not imply
assertions.note: Claim boundary check notes observational design cannot imply causality.

assertions.text: Section G explains why primary result should precede subgroup analyses
assertions.note: Structuring logic explicitly states subgroups derive meaning from confirmed primary finding.

inputs.label: GWAS study with primary locus identification, fine-mapping, functional annotation, and external replication
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. GWAS-to-validation hierarchy correctly constructed. Fine-mapping correctly placed before functional annotation.

assertions.text: Output orders results as GWAS discovery → fine-mapping → functional annotation → replication, not chronologically
assertions.note: results-ordering-rules.md applied — evidentiary function order, not analysis timeline.

assertions.text: Output treats external replication as a validation layer, not a secondary finding
assertions.note: Replication placed after functional annotation, correctly positioned as confirmation layer.

assertions.text: Output does not promote functional annotation to primary result status
assertions.note: Hard rule 4 holds — functional annotation is mechanistic support, not primary claim.

assertions.text: Section H states that GWAS results support association, not causation
assertions.note: Claim boundary check correctly flags that functional annotation supports plausibility, not mechanistic proof.

assertions.text: Section E defines distinct paragraph roles for each of the four result layers
assertions.note: Each layer has a stated purpose distinguishing it from the others.

inputs.label: User provides only study topic ('a study of gut microbiome in IBD') with no figure inventory
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Clarification-first gate + upload recommendation triggered correctly. No fabricated structure produced.

assertions.text: Output triggers clarification-first gate and requests figure inventory before structuring
assertions.note: Hard rule 2 applied — no long Results structure built without result hierarchy information.

assertions.text: Output invokes upload-recommendation-rule.md and recommends uploading figure list or results report
assertions.note: Specific upload recommendation: figure legend list, analysis outline, or results report.

assertions.text: Output does not fabricate a Results structure from topic alone
assertions.note: Hard rule 1 holds — no invented figure order produced.

assertions.text: Section I lists specific missing inputs that would enable a real structuring
assertions.note: Figure inventory, primary finding identification, study design type all listed.

assertions.text: Output explains why topic-only input is insufficient for structuring
assertions.note: Structuring requires knowing what figures exist and which is the primary result.

inputs.label: RCT manuscript with primary endpoint correctly placed but 6 secondary endpoints and subgroups disordered
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. CONSORT-informed ordering applied. Secondary endpoints grouped before subgroup analyses. Adverse events correctly placed last.

assertions.text: Output preserves the correctly placed primary endpoint and only reorganizes secondary content
assertions.note: Primary endpoint position retained; secondary disorder diagnosed and corrected.

assertions.text: Output recommends grouping secondary endpoints before subgroup analyses
assertions.note: Secondary outcomes are pre-specified; subgroups are exploratory — ordering distinction correctly applied.

assertions.text: Output places adverse events reporting after all efficacy results
assertions.note: CONSORT-consistent ordering: safety results after all efficacy content.

assertions.text: Section H states that subgroup analyses are exploratory and must not be implied as confirmatory
assertions.note: Claim boundary check explicitly prevents misrepresentation of post-hoc subgroups.

assertions.text: Output does not invent additional secondary endpoints to fill gaps in the structure
assertions.note: Hard rule 1 holds — structure built from the six provided secondary endpoints only.

inputs.label: Multi-omics study with RNA-seq, proteomics, and single-cell data across 12 figures
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Multi-omics integration order correctly applied. Figures grouped by analytical layer, not by data modality sequence.

assertions.text: Output groups figures by evidentiary function (primary, corroboration, mechanistic, validation) not by data type
assertions.note: RNA-seq primary finding leads; proteomics placed as corroboration; single-cell used for mechanistic layer.

assertions.text: Output identifies which of the 12 figures are primary vs supporting
assertions.note: Section B and Section D explicitly label each figure's role in the hierarchy.

assertions.text: Output recommends uploading study protocol when analytical hierarchy is ambiguous across modalities
assertions.note: Upload recommendation invoked for cross-modality ordering decisions requiring primary endpoint specification.

assertions.text: Section H states that multi-omics corroboration does not constitute mechanistic proof
assertions.note: Claim boundary check flags association vs. causal mechanism distinction for multi-omics.

assertions.text: Output does not treat all 12 figures as equally weighted primary results
assertions.note: Hard rule 5 holds — figure hierarchy established, figure-by-figure dump avoided.

inputs.label: User asks to write the full Results prose section and add Discussion-style interpretation within Results
inputs.note: All four assertions passed. Prose writing declined (redirects to results-section-writer). Discussion interpretation in Results declined per results-boundary-rules.md.

assertions.text: Output declines full prose writing as outside structurer scope and redirects to results-section-writer
assertions.note: Skill boundary correctly enforced — this skill structures, results-section-writer writes prose.

assertions.text: Output declines adding Discussion-style interpretation within Results section
assertions.note: results-boundary-rules.md applied — observed findings vs. speculative interpretation kept separate.

assertions.text: Output offers to produce a structure outline that can then be handed to results-section-writer
assertions.note: Constructive redirect with explicit composability pathway described.

assertions.text: Output explains why Results/Discussion boundary matters for peer-review credibility
assertions.note: Reviewers flag overinterpretation in Results as a structural deficiency — clearly explained.

inputs.label: User insists three exploratory post-hoc analyses should be the primary result of the paper
inputs.note: All five assertions passed. Hard rule 4 applied. Exploratory analyses correctly demoted to post-hoc section. Section H flags the evidence-level mismatch.

assertions.text: Output refuses to promote exploratory analyses to primary result status
assertions.note: Hard rule 4 explicitly applied — exploratory analyses cannot be presented as primary findings.

assertions.text: Output recommends a dedicated 'Exploratory/Post-hoc analyses' subsection for these results
assertions.note: Constructive alternative provided: separate subsection with explicit exploratory labeling.

assertions.text: Section H flags that presenting exploratory analyses as primary results misleads reviewers about pre-specification
assertions.note: Claim boundary check explains risk: reviewers will question why these were not pre-specified.

assertions.text: Output asks the user to identify what the pre-specified primary outcome is
assertions.note: Clarification-first applied: user must confirm the actual primary endpoint before structuring proceeds.

assertions.text: Output does not produce a fraudulent structure placing exploratory analyses in the primary result position
assertions.note: Hard rule 10 holds — no structure implying stronger evidence chain than results support.

key_strengths: Citation-support annotation with PubMed search queries and explicit opt-out provides literature anchoring without fabricating references — a uniquely safe implementation
Section H (Claim Boundary Check) is a dedicated output section that makes evidence-level constraints explicit — most writing skills lack this as a mandatory output
Ten study types covered including single-cell, multi-omics, and MR/QTL — broader scope than typical Results-structuring tools
Results-ordering-rules.md frames ordering by 'narrative and evidentiary function, not chronological analysis order' — precisely correct distinction that prevents common fragmentation
Upload-recommendation-rule.md provides a specific protocol-upload pathway when figure inventory is insufficient — prevents fabricated structuring from incomplete input

recommendations.title: No explicit composability with results-section-writer
recommendations.problem: The skill handles the out-of-scope prose request in Input 6 by declining, but does not explicitly document in SKILL.md that it is the upstream component of a results-section-structurer → results-section-writer pipeline.
recommendations.root_cause: Skill designed as standalone without explicit upstream/downstream positioning.
recommendations.fix: Add to SKILL.md scope boundary: 'This skill produces the Results section structure. Use results-section-writer for full prose generation from the approved structure. Output from this skill should be used as input for results-section-writer.'

recommendations.title: No guidance on supplementary vs main-text figure placement
recommendations.problem: The skill structures main-text figures but provides no guidance on which figures should be demoted to supplementary materials. This is a common structuring decision in omics and multi-panel studies.
recommendations.root_cause: Scope covers figure ordering in Results but not the main-text vs. supplementary allocation decision.
recommendations.fix: Add to results-ordering-rules.md: 'When figure count exceeds 8 or analytical breadth creates narrative congestion, recommend which figures are supplementary candidates. Validation sub-analyses and sensitivity analysis details are typical supplementary candidates.'

recommendations.title: Hard rule 8 (citation-support annotation) is unclear in a structuring context
recommendations.problem: Hard rule 8 mandates citation-support markers for context-setting statements, but the skill is structuring an outline, not writing prose. It is unclear when citation markers should appear in an outline vs. prose draft.
recommendations.root_cause: Citation annotation rule was written for prose contexts and may not translate cleanly to an outline format.
recommendations.fix: Clarify in citation-support-annotation-rules.md: 'When producing a Results outline, add citation markers and PubMed queries to Section F only for statements that would appear as introductory context sentences in the prose draft, not for every result description.'

recommendations.title: No guidance on figure count or subsection count targets
recommendations.problem: When structuring a 12-figure multi-omics paper, the skill creates a valid structure but provides no guidance on whether the resulting number of subsections is appropriate for a typical journal Results section.
recommendations.root_cause: Results-ordering-rules.md addresses order but not scale.
recommendations.fix: Add to results-ordering-rules.md: 'A typical Results section for a high-impact journal has 4–7 subsections and 6–10 main-text figures. Flag when a proposed structure significantly exceeds these ranges and suggest condensation or supplementary reassignment.'
